meta.title: 9.5 CRDTs and Conflict Resolution
meta.description: Conflict-free replicated data types, last-writer-wins, merge functions, and designing for concurrent updates.

blocks.prompt: Two users simultaneously edit the same document — one on a plane (offline) and one in an office. When the plane lands and syncs, how do you merge their changes without losing either person's work?

blocks.content:
## Conflict resolution strategies

### Last-Writer-Wins (LWW)
Use timestamps to pick the 'latest' write. Simple but loses data — the earlier write is silently discarded.

### Application-level merge
Custom logic combines concurrent writes. Complex but preserves all data. Example: merging shopping carts = union of items.

### CRDTs (Conflict-free Replicated Data Types)
Data structures mathematically guaranteed to converge when replicas merge, regardless of operation order.

**Types:**
- **G-Counter:** Grow-only counter. Each node increments its own counter. Total = sum of all counters.
- **PN-Counter:** Positive-Negative counter. Two G-Counters (one for increments, one for decrements).
- **G-Set:** Grow-only set. Elements can be added, never removed. Merge = union.
- **OR-Set (Observed-Remove Set):** Elements can be added and removed. Uses unique tags per add to resolve add/remove conflicts.
- **LWW-Register:** Last-writer-wins register. Simple but lossy.

**Used by:** Redis (CRDT-based active-active), Riak, Apple Notes (collaboration), Figma (multiplayer editing).

### When to use CRDTs

- Offline-first applications (mobile, collaborative editing)
- Multi-region active-active databases
- Systems where availability > consistency
- When you can express your data model as CRDT operations

CRDTs are data structures that automatically converge without coordination — ideal for multi-region and offline-first systems. LWW is simplest but loses data. Application-level merge preserves data but is complex. Choose based on your data model and conflict tolerance.

blocks.prompt: Module 9.6 covers consistent hashing — how to distribute data across nodes while minimizing redistribution when nodes change.
